questions.stem: You have just finished implementing a change and your local code passes your own tests. In the conclusion phase, what is your very first action?

options.text: Schedule the next acceptance-testing session with stakeholders
Delete the obsolete tests from the suite
Commit your updated code to the configuration-management repository

questions.explanation: The conclusion begins with commit: returning updated code to the CM repository (resolving conflicts). Baseline, acceptance, and release all come after commit, and deleting tests is test-suite maintenance, not the first conclusion action. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: You are concluding a change and your commit lands minutes before nightly baseline testing kicks off. A teammate committed an overlapping edit an hour earlier. What must you do as part of committing?

options.text: Ask stakeholders to approve the merge
Re-run concept location on the conflicting file
Resolve the conflict with the teammate's already-committed change
Wait for the next release before committing

questions.explanation: Commit means returning code to the shared CM repository while resolving conflicts with whatever others committed in the meantime. You do not wait for a release, re-run concept location, or seek stakeholder approval to merge. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: Your committed code has been integrated and you want it to become the team's agreed reference version. Which conclusion action achieves that?

options.text: Creating a new baseline through thorough testing
Pushing a patch via the merge tool

questions.explanation: A new baseline is created by thorough team-wide testing that certifies the integrated code as the new agreed-upon version. Release ships to users, acceptance is the stakeholder gate, and a patch is a delivery mechanism. (Conclusion p.2-3)

questions.stem: As the developer who just concluded the owner change, who actually delivers your baseline code to end users?

options.text: You commit it directly to users
The merge tool does it automatically at commit
The testing team installs it during baseline testing
It is delivered as a release, a separate later step

questions.explanation: Delivering baseline code to users is the release step, which happens from time to time and is separate from your commit. Commit only reaches the team; baseline testing certifies, it does not deliver. (Conclusion p.2, p.9)

questions.stem: You committed on time, baseline testing passed, but your change still is not on users' machines. As the concluding developer, what remains to be done?

options.text: Re-run unit tests, since baseline does not test units
Nothing; baseline certification ships to users automatically
Substantial extra work to package and release it to users

questions.explanation: Even after a clean baseline, reaching users requires a release, which needs substantial extra work and is a technical-and-business decision. Baseline certification does not auto-ship, and concept classification is long past. (Conclusion p.9)

questions.stem: You are concluding a tiny bug fix for an app on a versioned-lifespan model. The fix is small and urgent. How is it most likely delivered to users?

options.text: By re-baselining the whole system overnight
As a patch merged into the user's program by the tool 'merge'
Through stakeholder acceptance only, with no delivery

questions.explanation: Small releases are delivered as patches incorporated into the user's program by the 'merge' tool. Large installers carry big releases; re-baselining and acceptance are not delivery mechanisms. (Conclusion p.10)

questions.stem: You are a programmer in a quarterly-release shop. You finish a change two days after baseline testing began. What is the realistic conclusion-phase outcome for you?

options.text: Your change is auto-merged because the shop uses CI
The testing team reverts the whole baseline to include you
Your change is excluded from this baseline; you re-integrate and submit by the next deadline, with extra work
Your change ships in this quarter's release regardless

questions.explanation: Missing the commit deadline (baseline-test start) means your work is not in this baseline; you must submit by the next deadline, doing extra re-integration work against a moved baseline, and management notes the miss. (Conclusion p.5)

questions.stem: At conclusion, stakeholders sit down to thoroughly exercise the new owner feature before approving it for users. Which activity is this?
questions.explanation: Stakeholders conducting functional testing that thoroughly exercises functionalities and approves software for release is acceptance testing. Baseline/unit/mutation testing are performed by the team or tools, not stakeholders. (Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: You are the release manager deciding how often to ship. The deck says this is which kind of decision?

options.text: Decided solely by the specialised testing team
Decided solely by the merge tool
Both a technical and a business decision

questions.explanation: Release frequency is both a technical and a business decision and requires substantial extra work. It is not purely technical, not tool-driven, and not owned by the testing team. (Conclusion p.9)

questions.stem: Concluding the owner change, you want the safest possible certification that the system has not regressed. Who runs that certification and when?

options.text: A specialised testing team, often overnight or over the weekend
You, in your IDE, in a few seconds
The merge tool, at patch time

questions.explanation: Baseline testing is run by a specialised testing team and is expensive, often executed overnight or over the weekend. It is not an instant IDE action, a stakeholder demo, or a merge-tool function. (Conclusion p.3)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the three steps of the conclusion phase?
questions.explanation: The three conclusion steps are commit, new baseline, and new release. Concept location is an early phase (finding where a concept lives), not part of conclusion. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: Which statement about a BROKEN baseline is NOT true?

options.text: The fixes are simply added to the bug stack and the baseline is still certified
The whole increment's work may be invalidated or postponed
Programmers who committed buggy files can be identified and their reputation suffers
The testing team rejects the buggy commits

questions.explanation: Adding fixes to the bug stack while still certifying is the bugs-in-baseline (minor) response, NOT broken baseline. Broken baseline means rejection, possible invalidation, and reputational harm. (Conclusion p.6-7)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a consequence of missing the baseline deadline?

options.text: You re-integrate against a now-moved baseline, sometimes significant extra work
Management knows how often you missed it and may ask for an explanation
Your work is excluded from this baseline
The whole team's increment is immediately invalidated

questions.explanation: Immediate invalidation of the whole increment is the broken-baseline consequence, not a missed-deadline one. Missing the deadline excludes only your work, costs re-integration effort, and is visible to management. (Conclusion p.5, p.7)

questions.stem: Which of these does a commit NOT do, in the conclusion phase?

options.text: Return updated code to the CM repository
Make the local change part of the shared trunk
Deliver the code to end users

questions.explanation: Commit hands code to the team, not users; delivery to users is the separate release step. Commit does return code to the CM repo, resolve conflicts, and join the shared trunk. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: Which concept from the owner change request is NOT classified as 'significant'?
questions.explanation: owner is classified as external (it crosses the system boundary). figure and canvas are the significant concepts already in the design. (Drawlets p.8)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT an 'external' concept in the Drawlets classification?
questions.explanation: figure is significant (already in the design), not external. owner, ID, and password are the external concepts that come from outside the system. (Drawlets p.8)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a new class added during actualization of the owner change?
questions.explanation: AbstractFigure already exists; it is the located origin class that the change modifies. OwnerIdentity and SimpleListener are the two genuinely new (blue-box) classes. (Drawlets p.16)

questions.stem: Which statement is NOT an accurate description of baseline testing?

options.text: It must guarantee progress, not regression
It is expensive and often run overnight by a specialised team
It can reveal bugs in faulty committed code
It rewrites the committed source code to fix bugs

questions.explanation: Baseline testing certifies; it does not rewrite source. It does guarantee progress-not-regression, is expensive, and surfaces bugs in faulty commits. (Conclusion p.3, p.6)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the two ways the deck says refactoring shortens change propagation?

options.text: Neither of the move nor split strategies
Deleting all regression tests to reduce the suite
Moving code affected by the change into fewer classes
Splitting the roles of an overloaded method

questions.explanation: The two strategies are moving code into fewer classes and splitting roles. Deleting regression tests is never proposed; unaffected tests are kept as regression tests. (Drawlets p.31, p.29)

questions.stem: Which statement about the conclusion phase is FALSE?

options.text: Baseline testing is internal; acceptance testing is external
The conclusion is finished the moment the developer commits
Commit, baseline, acceptance, and release are widening gates
Release frequency is a technical and business decision

questions.explanation: The conclusion is NOT finished at commit; commit only reaches the team, and baseline, acceptance, and release follow. The other statements are all accurate. (Conclusion p.2, p.8-9)

questions.stem: During test-suite maintenance after a change, which action is NOT performed?

options.text: Add tests for the new features
Delete all tests that the change touched
Keep unaffected old tests as regression tests

questions.explanation: Tests impacted by the change are UPDATED, not deleted. Maintenance keeps unaffected tests as regression, removes obsolete ones, and adds new-feature tests. (Drawlets p.28-29)

questions.stem: Which is NOT true of concept location as shown on Drawlets?

options.text: It is a simple table lookup with no exploration
Dependency direction decides which candidate is correct
It is a graph search that can take a wrong turn
It requires backtracking from dead-end candidates

questions.explanation: Concept location is search, not lookup: it forms hypotheses, tests them, and backtracks. The wrong-way/right-way story (container vs. abstraction) shows direction matters. (Drawlets p.9-15)

questions.stem: Compare 'bugs in baseline' with 'broken baseline'. Which contrast is correct?

options.text: Both reject the commits and differ only in severity wording
Bugs-in-baseline still certifies and defers fixes to the stack; broken-baseline rejects commits and may invalidate the increment
Both certify the baseline and differ only in who is blamed
Bugs-in-baseline rejects the increment; broken-baseline defers fixes

questions.explanation: Minor bugs: certify and defer to the bug stack. Serious bugs: reject and possibly invalidate the increment. The other options invert or conflate the two responses. (Conclusion p.6-7)

questions.stem: How does a commit differ from a new baseline?

options.text: Commit certifies the whole system; baseline hands code to one developer
Commit hands code to the team; baseline certifies the integrated whole as the new reference version
They are two names for the same step
Commit ships to users; baseline is internal only

questions.explanation: Commit returns one developer's code to the team's repo; baseline certifies the integrated whole as the agreed reference. Commit does not certify the whole system or ship to users. (Conclusion p.2-3)

questions.stem: Contrast a large release with a small release in delivery terms.

options.text: Large = merged patch; small = downloaded installer
Large = downloaded and installed; small = patch merged via 'merge'
Both are downloaded installers; only frequency differs
Both are merged patches; only size differs

questions.explanation: Large releases are downloaded and installed; small releases are patches merged into the user's program by 'merge'. Swapping the two delivery methods, or claiming both use the same method, is wrong. (Conclusion p.10)

questions.stem: Contrast baseline testing with acceptance testing.

options.text: Baseline = stakeholders, external; acceptance = testing team, internal
Baseline = specialised testing team, internal certification; acceptance = stakeholders, external approval for release
Both are run by stakeholders before commit
Both are run by the merge tool at release

questions.explanation: Baseline testing is the internal certification by the testing team; acceptance testing is the external gate by stakeholders that approves release. The other options swap roles or invent actors. (Conclusion p.3, p.8)

questions.stem: Compare the 'move the code' refactoring with 'splitting the roles' on the owner change.

options.text: Move splits move into two methods; splitting pulls code into ConstructionTool
Move pulls duplicated move-logic up into ConstructionTool; splitting divides move into move and secureMove
Both add a third new class to the design
Both leave the classes-modified count unchanged at 13

questions.explanation: Move-the-code extracts moveFigure and pulls it up into ConstructionTool; splitting divides AbstractFigure.move into move and secureMove. Both still add only 2 classes, and both REDUCE classes modified (8 and 5). (Drawlets p.33, p.36-37)

questions.stem: On Drawlets, contrast the 'wrong way' with the 'right way' in concept location.

options.text: Both land on SimpleApplet; only the colour differs
Wrong way uses ID; right way uses password
Wrong way goes up to the abstraction; right way goes to the container
Wrong way goes to the container (SimpleDrawingCanvas); right way goes up to the abstraction (AbstractFigure)

questions.explanation: The wrong way followed the dependency down to the container SimpleDrawingCanvas; the right way went up to the figure abstraction AbstractFigure, where per-figure state lives. (Drawlets p.10-15)

questions.stem: Compare a missed deadline with a broken baseline in terms of what is lost.

options.text: Both always cost the same re-integration work
Missed deadline loses the whole increment; broken baseline loses one developer's change
Missed deadline loses only your slot in this baseline; broken baseline can invalidate the whole increment and harm reputations
Both lose nothing; both are auto-corrected

questions.explanation: A missed deadline excludes only your work (wait for next baseline); a broken baseline can invalidate the whole increment and damage reputations. The scope of loss is the key contrast. (Conclusion p.5, p.7)

questions.stem: Contrast unit tests with functional tests as reported for Drawlets.

options.text: 385 unit tests (1369 assertions) vs 141 scenario-style functional test cases
Both number 385; only the tooling differs
Unit tests are run by stakeholders; functional tests by developers

questions.explanation: Drawlets has 385 unit tests with 1369 assertions and 141 functional (scenario-style: Draw/Select/Move) test cases. The numbers are not equal and the actors are not reversed. (Drawlets p.25-26)

questions.stem: Compare the audiences reached by commit, baseline, and release.

options.text: Commit -> users; baseline -> team; release -> project
Commit -> stakeholders; baseline -> users; release -> team
Commit -> team; baseline -> project; release -> users

questions.explanation: Commit hands code to the team, baseline certifies it for the project, release ships it to users - each a wider blast radius. The other orderings scramble this. (Conclusion p.2, p.9)

questions.stem: Compare 'classes modified' with 'LOC modified' across the three strategies (no-refactor / move / split).

options.text: Classes fall sharply (13->8->5) while LOC stays nearly flat (91->95->87)
Both stay constant across all three strategies
Classes stay flat while LOC falls sharply
Both fall sharply: classes 13->5 and LOC 91->50

questions.explanation: Refactoring roughly halves classes modified (13->8->5) but barely moves LOC (91->95->87), showing the benefit is shorter propagation, not fewer typed lines. (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: Contrast the internal vs external nature of the conclusion's two test gates.

options.text: Acceptance is internal; baseline is external
Baseline certification is internal (team); acceptance is external (stakeholders)

questions.explanation: Baseline testing certifies internally via the testing team; acceptance testing is the external stakeholder gate. The layering is internal-then-external. (Conclusion p.3, p.8)

questions.stem: Action: 'The maintainer follows dependencies from SimpleApplet, rejects SimpleDrawingCanvas, and lands on AbstractFigure.' Which phase is this?
questions.explanation: Searching the class graph to find where 'figure properties' live (with a backtrack to AbstractFigure) is concept location. Impact analysis estimates the ripple after the origin is found. (Drawlets p.9-15)

questions.stem: Action: 'OwnerIdentity and SimpleListener are introduced and the edit at AbstractFigure is propagated class-by-class.' Which phase is this?
questions.explanation: Implementing new classes and propagating the change across the graph is actualization. Concept location only located the origin; postfactoring is the later refactoring study. (Drawlets p.16-24)

questions.stem: Action: 'After living through a long propagation, the team extracts moveFigure and splits move/secureMove to make the next change cheaper.' Which phase?
questions.explanation: Refactoring performed after the change to shorten future propagation is postfactoring. Prefactoring would precede the change; the Drawlets study presents these after actualization. (Drawlets p.31-38)

questions.stem: Action: 'Estimate which classes the owner-check will touch before any code is written - predicting the tool tree, canvas, and palettes.' Which phase?
questions.explanation: Predicting the blast radius before coding is impact analysis. Concept location found the origin; actualization is the actual implementation. (Drawlets p.16-24 retro; impact analysis section)

questions.stem: Action: 'A request exists to add an owner per figure and it is judged worth doing for cooperative work.' Which phase?
questions.explanation: Recognising that a worth-doing change request exists is initiation. The cooperative-work rationale is the business case that justifies initiating. (Drawlets p.6)

questions.stem: Action: 'Run 385 unit tests and 141 functional tests, add tests for new classes, and keep unaffected tests as regression.' Which phase?
questions.explanation: Testing throughout and after the change is verification, the band that wraps every phase. Impact analysis and concept location precede coding. (Drawlets p.25-29)

questions.stem: Action: 'Return the finished code to the CM repository, certify a new baseline overnight, then release to users.' Which phase?
questions.explanation: Commit -> baseline -> release is the conclusion phase. Actualization implemented the change; prefactoring (if any) precedes it. (Conclusion p.2-10)

questions.stem: Action: 'Extract the nouns/verbs from the request, then sort them into irrelevant / external / significant.' Which step of which phase?

options.text: Concept extraction and classification within Concept Location

questions.explanation: Extracting concepts and classifying them into irrelevant/external/significant are the opening steps of concept location. Impact, commit, and postfactoring are different phases. (Drawlets p.7-8)

questions.stem: Action: 'SimpleApplet itself turns red because it manages sessions and IDs and therefore needs changing.' Which phase produced this marking?
questions.explanation: Marking classes red as the inconsistency wave reaches them (SimpleApplet needing session/ID changes) is part of change propagation during actualization. (Drawlets p.19)

questions.stem: Action: 'Stakeholders thoroughly exercise the owner feature and approve it for shipping.' Which phase and step?
questions.explanation: Stakeholders functionally exercising the feature and approving release is acceptance testing, a step within the conclusion. It is not unit testing, propagation, or impact estimation. (Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: Cause: a programmer commits faulty code with only a minor cosmetic bug. Consequence?

options.text: The release is cancelled by stakeholders
The increment is rejected and invalidated
The baseline is still certified and the fix is pushed onto the bug stack

questions.explanation: Minor bugs (bugs-in-baseline) still let the testing team certify the baseline; corrections go onto the bug-report stack for future changes. Rejection/invalidation is the serious (broken-baseline) path. (Conclusion p.6)

questions.stem: Cause: baseline testing is postponed for many weeks. Consequence?

options.text: Bugs accumulate, making the eventual testing task harder

questions.explanation: Postponing too long lets bugs accumulate, making the eventual test job harder. Overhead is the opposite failure (testing too often); tooling and rights are unaffected. (Conclusion p.4)

questions.stem: Cause: a team runs baseline testing several times a day. Consequence?
questions.explanation: Testing too frequently creates unnecessary overhead. Bug accumulation is the too-rare failure mode; frequency does not invalidate increments or delete tests. (Conclusion p.4)

questions.stem: Cause: a serious defect is found during baseline testing of the new increment. Consequence?

options.text: The fix is deferred to the bug stack and the baseline is certified
The deadline is extended for everyone
The testing team rejects the commits; the whole increment may be invalidated or postponed
Stakeholders immediately release it as a patch

questions.explanation: A serious defect means a broken baseline: commits rejected, increment possibly invalidated/postponed - a significant cost on large projects. Defer-and-certify is the minor case. (Conclusion p.7)

questions.stem: Cause: a developer repeatedly commits files that break the baseline. Consequence beyond schedule cost?

options.text: They can be identified and their reputation suffers
They gain priority in the next release

questions.explanation: On a broken baseline the team can identify who committed buggy files, and their reputation suffers. The deck mentions no auto-generated tests, baseline deletion, or release priority. (Conclusion p.7)

questions.stem: Cause: a developer misses the moment baseline testing starts. Consequence?

options.text: Their work is released as a patch immediately
Their work is auto-merged into the running baseline
Their work is not in this baseline; they submit by the next deadline

questions.explanation: The start of baseline testing is a hard commit deadline; missing it excludes the work from this baseline, forcing resubmission at the next one. It is not auto-merged, released, or discarded. (Conclusion p.5)

questions.stem: Cause: every creation tool duplicates the 'move it if at the wrong location' logic in basicNewFigure. Consequence the move-refactoring delivers?

options.text: The LOC modified drops to zero
The owner-check must touch all 13 classes again
The duplicated logic is extracted into moveFigure and pulled up into ConstructionTool, shrinking the impacted set
The two new classes are removed

questions.explanation: Extracting the duplicated move logic into moveFigure and pulling it up into ConstructionTool shrinks the impacted set to ConstructionTool plus four creation tools. LOC stays ~95; the new classes remain. (Drawlets p.33-34)

questions.stem: Cause: the splitting refactoring divides move into move and secureMove. Consequence the deck flags as a downside?

options.text: A new set of unit tests must be created and test code is duplicated

questions.explanation: Splitting touches the fewest classes (5) but a new set of unit tests must be created (test code duplicated), and writing new tests costs more than adapting old ones. LOC stays ~87. (Drawlets p.37-38)

questions.stem: Cause: a change passes baseline testing but stakeholders reject it at acceptance. Consequence?

options.text: Release is blocked even though the internal baseline was certified
The baseline is automatically broken and invalidated
Commit rights are revoked from the team
The change is re-routed to concept location

questions.explanation: Acceptance is the external gate to approve release; failing it blocks release even though baseline certification (internal) passed. It does not break the baseline or revoke rights. (Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: Cause: an owner-check is added to figure modification at AbstractFigure. Consequence for the class graph?

options.text: Only AbstractFigure changes; no other class is affected
The inconsistency propagates outward to tools, canvas, and palettes until consistency is restored
Concept location must be re-run from scratch

questions.explanation: One edit at AbstractFigure makes neighbours inconsistent, propagating through the upper graph (tools, canvas, palettes) until no inconsistencies remain. It is neither isolated nor a rewrite. (Drawlets p.16-24)

questions.stem: Your change is complete and locally tested. Which is the MOST appropriate next action in the conclusion?

options.text: Commit to the CM repository, resolving any conflicts
Email stakeholders to schedule acceptance testing

questions.explanation: Commit is the first conclusion step. Acceptance and release come later; impact analysis is long past. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: The testing team finds two trivial cosmetic glitches during baseline testing of an otherwise-solid increment. What is the MOST appropriate decision?

options.text: Reject all commits and invalidate the increment
Certify the baseline and log the glitches on the bug stack for future fixing

questions.explanation: Trivial bugs are 'bugs in baseline': still certify and defer fixes to the bug stack. Rejection/invalidation is reserved for serious (broken-baseline) defects. (Conclusion p.6)

questions.stem: A small urgent fix must reach users who already run the app. What is the MOST appropriate delivery?

options.text: A stakeholder demo with no delivery
A patch merged into the user's program via 'merge'
A full re-baseline pushed to each user

questions.explanation: Small releases ship as patches merged into the user's program by the 'merge' tool. Large installers are for big releases; re-baselining and demos are not delivery. (Conclusion p.10)

questions.stem: You must find where a per-figure 'owner' property should attach. What is the MOST appropriate class to anchor the search on?

options.text: figure, the significant concept, leading to AbstractFigure

questions.explanation: Anchor on the significant concept 'figure', which leads to AbstractFigure where per-figure state lives. owner/password are external; merge is unrelated. (Drawlets p.8, p.15)

questions.stem: A change just propagated across 13 classes. What is the MOST appropriate structural response to make the next owner-related change cheaper?

options.text: Refactor: move code into fewer classes and/or split overloaded method roles

questions.explanation: A long propagation is a signal to refactor (move code into fewer classes, split roles) so the next change is shorter. Deleting tests or skipping baselines is never the answer. (Drawlets p.31)

questions.stem: Management wants the conclusion to balance bug-accumulation against testing overhead. What is the MOST appropriate lever to tune?

options.text: The number of stakeholders at acceptance
The frequency of creating new baselines, set by program size and required quality
The colour code on the class diagram

questions.explanation: Baseline frequency is the tuning lever: too rare accumulates bugs, too frequent adds overhead; it is driven by program size and required quality. The other items do not trade off these costs. (Conclusion p.4)

questions.stem: After a change, an old test still exercises functionality the change did not touch. What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.text: Keep it as a regression test
Mark it red in the propagation diagram
Convert it to an acceptance test

questions.explanation: Unaffected old tests are kept as regression tests to guard future changes. Deletion is for obsolete tests; conversion and colouring are not maintenance actions. (Drawlets p.29)

questions.stem: A teammate insists 'committing to Git ends the change.' What is the BEST correction?

options.text: Tell them to re-run concept location
Explain that commit only reaches the team; baseline certification, acceptance, and release still follow
Agree; commit is the final step
Tell them to delete the bug stack

questions.explanation: Commit is one of several widening gates; baseline, acceptance, and release remain. The trap is treating commit as 'done'. (Conclusion p.2, p.8-9)

questions.stem: Given the test-cost caveat, which refactoring is the BEST choice is not obvious. Which reasoning is soundest?

options.text: Weigh fewer classes modified against the extra cost of creating duplicated new tests; fewest classes is not automatically least total effort
Pick whichever adds the most new classes
Always pick splitting; fewest classes always means least effort
Always pick no-refactoring; it modifies the fewest LOC

questions.explanation: Splitting modifies the fewest classes (5) but duplicates test code and costs more to write new tests; the deck warns fewest-classes is not automatically least total effort. (Drawlets p.37-38)

questions.stem: You want the safest sign that the integrated system has progressed without regressing before involving stakeholders. Which gate is MOST appropriate?

options.text: A certified new baseline from the specialised testing team
A single developer's local test run

questions.explanation: Baseline testing certifies progress-not-regression internally before stakeholders run acceptance. A local run is not team-wide; release and patches come later. (Conclusion p.3, p.8)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'The conclusion phase is just committing to Git.' What is the correction?

options.text: The conclusion is only the release
Correct; commit is the whole conclusion
The conclusion is only baseline testing
Commit is one of three steps; baseline certification and release also occur, plus stakeholder acceptance

questions.explanation: The conclusion is commit + baseline + release (with stakeholder acceptance gating release); it is not 'just committing'. (Conclusion p.2, p.8-10)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Verification is one final step at the very end.' What is the correction?

options.text: Verification is a band that wraps every phase, embodied throughout by the test suite
Verification is identical to acceptance testing
Correct; verification runs only after release
Verification is performed only by stakeholders

questions.explanation: Verification is the vertical band wrapping every phase, concretely the test suite running throughout, not one final step. It is broader than stakeholder acceptance. (Drawlets p.25)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Whether a baseline is broken depends on how many lines were changed.' What is the correction?

options.text: It depends on bug SEVERITY, judged by the testing team, not LOC count
Correct; more LOC means broken baseline
It depends on which programmer committed first
It depends on whether prefactoring was done

questions.explanation: The bugs-in-baseline vs broken-baseline split is a severity judgement by the testing team. LOC count, commit order, and prefactoring do not decide it. (Conclusion p.6-7)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'A change's cost equals the number of lines you finally type.' What is the correction?

options.text: Cost equals the number of stakeholders
The real cost is the size of the propagation triggered; Drawlets changed 0.5% of LOC yet rippled through ~13 classes
Cost equals the number of figure types
Correct; LOC is the true cost

questions.explanation: Cost is propagation size, not edit size: a 0.5% production-LOC edit propagated across ~13 classes. Stakeholder and figure-type counts are irrelevant to change cost. (Drawlets p.30, p.37)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Concept location is just looking the answer up in the code.' What is the correction?

options.text: Correct; it is a direct lookup
It is the same as impact analysis
It is a graph search with hypotheses and backtracking; expect a wrong turn

questions.explanation: Concept location is search, not lookup: form a hypothesis, test it, backtrack on dead ends. It is a developer activity distinct from impact analysis. (Drawlets p.9-15)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'More baseline testing is always better.' What is the correction?

options.text: Baseline testing should never be repeated
Correct; test as often as possible
Baseline testing is optional for large programs
It is a trade-off: too rare accumulates bugs, too frequent adds overhead

questions.explanation: Baseline frequency is a deliberate trade-off (bug accumulation vs overhead), not 'more is always better'. It is neither one-shot nor optional for large programs. (Conclusion p.4)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Splitting wins because it modifies the fewest classes (5).' What is the correction?

options.text: Splitting actually modifies the most classes
Splitting removes the two new classes
Splitting duplicates test code and costs more to write new tests, so it is not automatically the least total effort

questions.explanation: Fewest classes modified is not automatically least effort: splitting duplicates test code and writing new tests costs more than adapting old ones. (Drawlets p.38)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Missing the baseline deadline is harmless - you just wait.' What is the correction?

options.text: It immediately invalidates the whole increment
It costs extra re-integration work against a moved baseline and is visible to management

questions.explanation: Missing the deadline is not neutral: re-integration against a moved baseline is extra (sometimes significant) work, and management tracks the miss. (Conclusion p.5)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'You can skip concept classification and just start coding.' What is the correction?

options.text: Without classifying concepts you would not know to anchor on 'figure' (significant) rather than 'owner' (external)

questions.explanation: Classification tells you which concept to anchor the search on - figure (significant), not owner (external). Skipping it means you do not know where to search. (Drawlets p.8)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'During propagation you only change production code; tests are untouched.' What is the correction?

options.text: Tests are deleted entirely after every change
Tests propagate too: update impacted tests, add tests for new classes/features, keep unaffected as regression, delete obsolete

questions.explanation: Tests propagate alongside code: impacted tests updated, new-class/new-feature tests added, unaffected kept as regression, obsolete removed. (Drawlets p.28-29)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'The same team runs baseline and acceptance testing.' What is the correction?

options.text: The specialised testing team certifies the baseline (internal); stakeholders run acceptance (external)
The change author runs both alone

questions.explanation: Baseline testing is by the specialised testing team; acceptance testing is by stakeholders - different actors at different (internal/external) layers. (Conclusion p.3, p.8)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'The Drawlets refactoring discussion is a prefactoring step done before the change.' What is the correction?

options.text: It is presented after the change (postfactoring) as analysis of how the propagation could have been shortened
It is part of concept location
It is part of the release step

questions.explanation: The refactoring study comes after actualization as postfactoring, showing how the propagation could have been shorter; the deck performed no separate prefactoring. (Drawlets p.31)

questions.stem: Within the conclusion, which step immediately PRECEDES 'new baseline'?
questions.explanation: Order is commit -> new baseline -> new release. Commit precedes baseline. Acceptance and release follow; concept location is far earlier. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: Within the conclusion, which step immediately FOLLOWS 'new baseline'?
questions.explanation: Order is commit -> new baseline -> new release; release follows baseline. Commit precedes it; concept location and impact analysis are earlier phases. (Conclusion p.2, p.9)

questions.stem: Which phase immediately precedes Conclusion in the SB5-MAI change process?
questions.explanation: Conclusion sits below Postfactoring in the phase diagram; Postfactoring (refactor after the change) precedes Conclusion. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: Order the early phases of the owner change correctly.
questions.explanation: The request is initiated, then concept location finds AbstractFigure, then impact analysis estimates the ripple. The other orderings misplace one of the three. (Drawlets p.6)

questions.stem: Place the four conclusion gates in order of increasing scope.
questions.explanation: Increasing scope runs commit (team) -> baseline (project) -> acceptance (stakeholders) -> release (users). The other orderings scramble the gates. (Conclusion p.2-10)

questions.stem: Order the eight Drawlets phases as the worked example follows them.

options.text: Initiation, Concept Location, Actualization, Impact Analysis, Postfactoring, Prefactoring, Conclusion, Verification
Initiation, Impact Analysis, Concept Location, Actualization, Conclusion, Prefactoring, Postfactoring, Verification
Concept Location, Initiation, Impact Analysis, Actualization, Prefactoring, Verification, Conclusion, Postfactoring
Initiation, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring, Conclusion, Verification

questions.explanation: The example runs Initiation -> Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Conclusion -> Verification (the band wrapping all). (Drawlets p.16)

questions.stem: In the propagation sequence, which comes first?

options.text: The frontier reaches the leaf figure tools (RectangleTool, EllipseTool)
The edit at AbstractFigure makes Figure inconsistent and the wave starts outward

questions.explanation: Propagation starts at AbstractFigure/Figure and points outward (p.17); only later does the frontier reach leaf tools (p.23) and finish (p.24). (Drawlets p.17-24)

questions.stem: In the propagation animation, the frontier reaches the leaf figure tools at which stage relative to SimpleApplet turning red?

options.text: The leaf tools are never reached
After SimpleApplet turns red (SimpleApplet at p.19, leaf tools at p.23)

questions.explanation: SimpleApplet turns red around Propagation-2 (p.19); the frontier reaches the leaf figure tools later, at Propagation-6 (p.23). (Drawlets p.19, p.23)

questions.stem: Which comes first in concept location on Drawlets?

options.text: Mark AbstractFigure red as the result
Extract and classify the request's concepts

questions.explanation: Concept location first extracts and classifies concepts (p.7-8); only then does the graph walk start, hit the wrong way, backtrack, and finally mark AbstractFigure. (Drawlets p.7-15)

questions.stem: Verification is positioned in the process as what, relative to the other phases?

options.text: A vertical band that wraps and spans every phase
A single step strictly after Conclusion

questions.explanation: Verification is the vertical band spanning every phase, not a single late step, the first phase, or a commit sub-step. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: Where does Conclusion sit relative to Verification in the phase diagram?

options.text: Verification is a sub-step inside Conclusion
Conclusion is the last phase, still spanned by the vertical Verification band

questions.explanation: Conclusion is the last phase, sitting below Postfactoring, still wrapped by the vertical Verification band that spans every phase. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: In the Drawlets owner change, which class is the located origin where per-figure state (the new owner) attaches?
questions.explanation: AbstractFigure holds per-figure properties and is the concept-location result, so the owner attaches there. SimpleDrawingCanvas is the wrongly-tried container; OwnerIdentity is a new support class. (Drawlets p.15-16)

questions.stem: In the move-the-code refactoring, into which base class is moveFigure pulled up?
questions.explanation: The duplicated move logic is extracted into moveFigure and pulled up into the base class ConstructionTool. AbstractFigure holds the move/secureMove split; the others are not the pull-up target. (Drawlets p.33)

questions.stem: After the move-the-code refactoring, the owner-check's impacted set shrinks to which classes?

options.text: ConstructionTool plus the four concrete creation tools (RectangleTool, EllipseTool, RectangularCreationTool, PG_RectImageTool)
Every palette and tool in the graph

questions.explanation: The refactoring-impact diagram shows the owner-check now touches only ConstructionTool plus the four concrete creation tools - far smaller than the full p.24 propagation. (Drawlets p.34)

questions.stem: Which two roles of AbstractFigure.move motivate the splitting refactoring?

options.text: Moving as requested by the user (needs identity check) and moving as part of new-figure creation (no check needed)
Drawing a figure and deleting a figure

questions.explanation: move serves two roles: a user-requested move that must check owner identity, and a creation-time move that needs no check; splitting into move/secureMove confines the owner-check. (Drawlets p.36)

questions.stem: Read the comparison table: which row stays the SAME across no-refactoring, move, and splitting?
questions.explanation: Classes added (2) and interfaces modified (1) are invariant across all three strategies; classes modified (13->8->5) and effectively test cost change, and LOC varies slightly (91/95/87). (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: How big is Drawlets overall, per the deck?
questions.explanation: >100 classes, 35 interfaces, ~40 000 LOC. The 13/91 figures describe the change, and 385/4800 are test counts, not the whole system. (Drawlets p.2)

questions.stem: Apply the numbers: the owner change modified 91 production LOC. What fraction of the 17 800-LOC production baseline is that?
questions.explanation: 91 of 17 800 production LOC is about 0.5%. The change modified 124 test LOC (2.5% of the 4800 test LOC). (Drawlets p.30)

questions.stem: In the owner change, what is the role of the new OwnerIdentity class?

options.text: It represents the user identity an owner carries

questions.explanation: OwnerIdentity is the new class representing the user identity an owner carries; SimpleListener wires it in via Observer. It is not a test runner, host, or merge tool. (Drawlets p.16)

questions.stem: The newly introduced SimpleListener class takes on which design-pattern role?

options.text: An Observer/listener wiring the identity in

questions.explanation: SimpleListener is an Observer/listener that wires the owner identity in - figures use Composite, tools use Strategy. (Drawlets p.16; JHotDraw connection)

questions.stem: Which tools are the leaf figure-creation tools the propagation finally reaches?
questions.explanation: The leaf creation tools are RectangleTool, EllipseTool, RectangularCreationTool, and PG_RectImageTool, reached at Propagation-6. The others are higher in the graph. (Drawlets p.23)

questions.stem: Applying the table's lesson: refactoring cuts classes-modified from 13 to 5 but LOC barely changes. What is the actionable takeaway?

options.text: Refactoring's payoff is shorter propagation (fewer classes touched), not fewer lines typed
Refactoring removes the need for a baseline
Refactoring always reduces total effort once tests are counted

questions.explanation: The payoff is shorter propagation (13->5 classes) even though LOC is flat; and once test duplication is counted, the fewest-classes option is not guaranteed least effort. (Drawlets p.37-38)

questions.stem: At the end of propagation, the deck colours several classes grey for checked-but-unchanged. Which set is grey?
questions.explanation: ToolBar, ToolPalette, CanvasTool, Locator, and SequenceOfFigures were checked but unchanged (grey). AbstractFigure/Figure/SimpleApplet changed (red); the new classes are blue. (Drawlets p.24)

questions.stem: Reading the propagation colour code: a class marked RED means what?

options.text: Changed / now inconsistent and needing attention

questions.explanation: Red = class changed / now inconsistent and needing attention. Green is checked-consistent, grey is touched-but-unchanged or abandoned, blue boxes are new classes. (Drawlets p.17)

questions.stem: Reading the colour code: a BLUE BOX in the actualization/propagation diagram means what?

options.text: A new class added by the change (e.g. OwnerIdentity, SimpleListener)

questions.explanation: Blue boxes are the new classes added by the change (OwnerIdentity, SimpleListener). Red = must change, green = consistent, grey = abandoned/unchanged. (Drawlets p.16)

questions.stem: In the concept-location colour code, what does a GREY class signify?
questions.explanation: Grey marks an abandoned/backtracked candidate (e.g. ToolPalette, SimpleDrawingCanvas after rejection). Red is the located concept; green is comprehended; blue is new. (Drawlets p.11, p.14)

questions.stem: In the Drawlets concept-location colour code, what does GREEN mean?
questions.explanation: Green = comprehended class. Red = located concept, grey = abandoned, blue = new. (Drawlets p.9)

questions.stem: On the propagation diagrams, an ORANGE node is standing in for what?

options.text: The current frontier node being worked

questions.explanation: Orange marks the current frontier node being worked during propagation. Green is reconciled, blue is new, and there is no 'deleted' colour. (Drawlets p.20)

questions.stem: Interpret the figures: production baseline 17 800 LOC, change 91 LOC; test baseline 4800 LOC, change 124 LOC. Which test-to-production modified-LOC ratio results?
questions.explanation: 124 modified test LOC over 91 modified production LOC is about 1.4 test-LOC per production-LOC; the test code changed more in percentage terms (2.5% vs 0.5%). (Drawlets p.30)

questions.stem: Interpret the unit-test figures: 385 unit tests carry how many assertions and how many LOC?
questions.explanation: 385 unit tests carry 1369 assertions across ~4800 LOC of unit-test code. 141 is the functional-test count; 91/17 800 are change/baseline production figures. (Drawlets p.25)

questions.stem: Read the comparison table row 'Classes modified': what are the three values across no-refactor / move / split?
questions.explanation: Classes modified is 13 / 8 / 5. 2/2/2 is classes added, 91/95/87 is LOC modified, and 1/1/1 is interfaces modified. (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: Read the comparison table row 'LOC modified': what are the three values?
questions.explanation: LOC modified is 91 / 95 / 87 - nearly flat. 13/8/5 is classes modified, 2/2/2 is classes added, and the last set are test counts. (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: Reading 'Propagation - done' (p.24): what does the final diagram show about the system state?

options.text: No inconsistencies remain: a red modified set, grey checked-unchanged classes, and two blue new classes

questions.explanation: At 'Propagation - done' no inconsistencies remain: a large red modified set, grey checked-but-unchanged classes, and the two blue new classes - the system is consistent again. (Drawlets p.24)

questions.stem: On 'Propagation - 3' (p.20), the palettes settle to GREY. Reading the colour code, what does that mean for them?

options.text: They were checked and confirmed unaffected by the change

questions.explanation: Grey here means the palettes were checked and confirmed unaffected (no change needed). Red would mean change-needed; blue is new; the located concept was AbstractFigure. (Drawlets p.20)

questions.stem: Reading the blue ARROWS in the propagation diagrams, what do they indicate?

options.text: The direction the inconsistency is propagating

questions.explanation: Blue arrows show the direction the inconsistency propagates through the graph. They are not deletions, coverage links, or release channels. (Drawlets p.17)

questions.stem: You are on the specialised testing team. The overnight run reveals the increment crashes on a core flow. What do you do?

options.text: Certify and add the crash to the bug stack
Reject the buggy commits; the increment may be invalidated or postponed

questions.explanation: A core crash is a serious defect: a broken baseline, so the team rejects the commits and the increment may be invalidated/postponed. Certify-and-defer is for minor bugs only. (Conclusion p.7)

questions.stem: As a stakeholder running acceptance tests, what two things do you accomplish?

options.text: Write unit tests and run them in CI
Resolve merge conflicts and certify the baseline
Gain progress information and decide whether to approve the software for release

questions.explanation: Acceptance testing gives stakeholders progress information and is the gate to approve release. Conflict resolution, certification, impact, and unit tests belong to other actors/phases. (Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: You start concept location at SimpleApplet because it is already comprehended. Why begin from a comprehended class?

options.text: Because a known anchor lets you follow dependencies into unknown code methodically
Because it is a new class
Because it holds the owner property
Because it is the located concept

questions.explanation: Starting from a comprehended class (SimpleApplet) gives a known anchor to follow dependencies into unfamiliar code; it is not the located concept (AbstractFigure) nor a new class. (Drawlets p.9)

questions.stem: You are advising a team that wants to ship features the instant they pass developer tests. Per the deck, what is the strongest objection?

options.text: Code must still pass team-wide baseline certification and stakeholder acceptance before users get it
The merge tool only works at midnight
Concept location must be re-run before each ship

questions.explanation: Beyond developer tests, code must pass baseline certification (testing team) and stakeholder acceptance before release to users - the conclusion's widening gates. (Conclusion p.2-9)

questions.stem: Which is NOT true of a release?

options.text: It is purely a technical decision with no business input
It delivers baseline code to users
Its frequency mixes large and small releases

questions.explanation: Release frequency is both a technical AND a business decision - not purely technical. It does deliver baseline code, needs substantial work, and mixes large/small releases. (Conclusion p.9)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a feature/fact the deck states about Drawlets?

options.text: It is marketed as a 'perfect API'
It was written by Beck and Cunningham and ported to Java
It is a relational database with stored procedures

questions.explanation: Drawlets is a drawing-canvas application framework, not a database. It does have >100 classes/35 interfaces, Beck & Cunningham origins, and the 'perfect API' label. (Drawlets p.1-2)

questions.stem: Which is NOT part of the conclusion's three logical steps even though it occurs in the conclusion phase?
questions.explanation: The three logical STEPS are commit, new baseline, new release. Acceptance testing is a stakeholder gate within conclusion but is not counted as one of the three commit/baseline/release steps. (Conclusion p.2, p.8)

questions.stem: Contrast 'bugs in baseline' and 'broken baseline' by who absorbs the cost.

options.text: Both only cost the original author
Bugs-in-baseline defers cost to future fixers (bug stack); broken-baseline imposes increment-wide cost and reputational harm
Both invalidate the increment for everyone

questions.explanation: Minor bugs push cost onto future fixers via the bug stack; a broken baseline imposes a project-wide cost (invalidated increment) plus reputational harm. (Conclusion p.6-7)

questions.stem: Contrast regression tests with obsolete tests in post-change maintenance.

options.text: Unaffected tests are kept as regression tests; obsolete tests are removed
Both are converted to acceptance tests
Regression tests are deleted; obsolete tests are kept

questions.explanation: Unaffected old tests become regression tests (kept); obsolete tests are removed; new-feature tests are added. (Drawlets p.29)

questions.stem: Action: 'Tests not affected by the change are retained to guard future edits.' Which test-maintenance category is this?
questions.explanation: Keeping unaffected tests to guard future changes defines regression tests. Obsolete tests are removed; new-feature tests are added for the change. (Drawlets p.29)

questions.stem: Action: 'The class graph is searched, a candidate is examined on the fly and rejected, and the walk backs up.' Which phase and behaviour?
questions.explanation: Examining a candidate on the fly, rejecting it, and backing up is the backtracking search of concept location. Impact analysis estimates; it does not backtrack class-by-class. (Drawlets p.10-14)

questions.stem: Cause: the testing team certifies a baseline with minor bugs logged to the stack. Consequence over time if the stack is ignored?

options.text: The release becomes a large installer
Deferred bug-fixes accumulate as technical debt to be paid in future changes

questions.explanation: Logging minor bugs to the stack preserves progress but accrues technical debt - fixes deferred to future changes. Ignoring the stack lets debt accumulate. (Conclusion p.6)

questions.stem: Cause: AbstractFigure.move is split into move and secureMove. Consequence for the creation path?

options.text: The creation path keeps the cheap move and stays unchanged, shortening propagation
The creation path becomes a new class
The creation path now also checks identity

questions.explanation: Splitting confines the owner-check to secureMove; the creation path keeps the cheap move and stays unchanged, which shortens propagation. (Drawlets p.36)

questions.stem: A change is concluded in a continuous-integration shop. What is the MOST accurate expectation versus a quarterly-release shop?

options.text: Stakeholders are removed from the process
The same three logical steps (commit, baseline, release) occur, but tooling and cadence differ

questions.explanation: Activities depend on the process, but the same three logical steps occur in both; only the tooling and cadence differ. Stakeholders and baselines are not removed. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Adding an owner is a one-class edit because it only changes AbstractFigure.' What is the correction?

options.text: It changes every class in the framework
The edit propagates across ~13 modified classes plus 2 new ones, even though it touches only 0.5% of production LOC

questions.explanation: Though the located origin is AbstractFigure, the change propagates across ~13 modified classes plus 2 new ones - cost is propagation, not the single origin. (Drawlets p.16-24, p.37)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'Baseline testing ships the code to users.' What is the correction?

options.text: Baseline testing certifies internally; a separate release step delivers to users
Baseline testing is run by users

questions.explanation: Baseline testing certifies the integrated whole internally; reaching users requires the separate release step. It is not run by users and does not create patches. (Conclusion p.3, p.9)

questions.stem: Where does Postfactoring sit relative to Actualization and Conclusion?
questions.explanation: Postfactoring (refactor after the change) follows Actualization and precedes Conclusion in the phase order. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: In the conclusion's audience progression, which audience is reached LAST?
questions.explanation: Commit reaches the team, baseline the project, acceptance the stakeholders, and release reaches users last. (Conclusion p.2-9)

questions.stem: Which tools are named as functional/acceptance-test machinery for Drawlets?

options.text: JGiven and Mockito (with a record/playback tool)

questions.explanation: Drawlets' functional tests run with JGiven and Mockito, and acceptance tests use a record/playback tool. The other tool pairs are not named. (Drawlets p.27)

questions.stem: Of the three refactoring strategies, which modifies the FEWEST classes, and how many?
questions.explanation: Splitting modifies the fewest classes (5), versus 8 for move-function and 13 for no-refactoring. The '2' is classes added, constant across all. (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: On Propagation-2 (p.19), SimpleApplet turns RED. Reading the code, why?

options.text: It manages sessions/IDs and therefore needs changing for owners
It was abandoned as a dead-end

questions.explanation: SimpleApplet turns red because it manages sessions and IDs, so it genuinely needs changing for the owner feature. Red = needs change, not new/abandoned/consistent. (Drawlets p.19)

questions.stem: At Propagation-5 (p.22) most of the upper graph has gone RED. By the colour key, what does that big red cluster stand for?

options.text: Classes that were checked and need no change
The classes that genuinely require edits for the owner change

questions.explanation: The large red set at Propagation-5 are the classes that genuinely require edits. Grey would be checked-unchanged; blue new; the abandoned candidates were in concept location. (Drawlets p.22)

questions.stem: What does the 'commit' step return code to?
questions.explanation: Commit returns updated code to the configuration-management repository (resolving conflicts). Users get it via release; the bug stack holds deferred fixes; acceptance is a stakeholder gate. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: A new release delivers code to which audience?
questions.explanation: A release delivers baseline code to users. The testing team and stakeholders are reached at earlier gates; the author committed long before. (Conclusion p.9)

questions.stem: Acceptance testing is conducted by whom?
questions.explanation: Acceptance testing is functional testing done by stakeholders. It is not automated tooling, CI, or the author. (Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: Drawlets adds what to its host application?
questions.explanation: Drawlets is an application framework that adds a drawing canvas (with figure types) to a host application. (Drawlets p.1)

questions.stem: The concept-location search for the owner change starts at which class?
questions.explanation: The search starts at the already-comprehended host class SimpleApplet, then follows dependencies. AbstractFigure is the result, not the start. (Drawlets p.9)

questions.stem: Concept location concludes by marking which class red as the result?
questions.explanation: The Summary slide marks AbstractFigure red as the class holding figure properties - the located concept. SimpleDrawingCanvas was the rejected container. (Drawlets p.15)

questions.stem: Which phase is the LAST phase of a software change?
questions.explanation: Conclusion is the last phase. Verification is a band wrapping every phase; actualization and concept location are earlier. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: Small releases are incorporated into the user's program by which tool?
questions.explanation: Small releases (patches) are merged into the user's program by the tool 'merge'. JGiven/Mockito are test tools. (Conclusion p.10)

questions.stem: The Drawlets functional-test suite (Draw/Select/Move scenarios) totals how many test cases?
questions.explanation: Drawlets has 141 functional test cases (Draw/Select/Move scenarios). 385 is unit tests, 1369 assertions, 91 the modified production LOC. (Drawlets p.26)

questions.stem: Drawlets' unit-test baseline comprises how many individual unit tests?
questions.explanation: 385 unit tests (with 1369 assertions across ~4800 LOC). 141 is functional tests; 13 is classes modified; 4800 is test LOC. (Drawlets p.25)

questions.stem: Why does the conclusion's concrete activity vary between teams?

options.text: Because the precise activities depend on the specific software process
Because baselines are illegal in some shops

questions.explanation: The deck states the precise conclusion activities depend on the specific software process; the same three logical steps still occur. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: In the worked example, how do 'new baseline' and 'new release' relate to one another?

options.text: Baseline is for users; release is for the team
Baseline certifies internally; release later delivers that certified baseline to users

questions.explanation: Baseline certifies the integrated whole internally; the release later delivers that certified baseline to users - release follows baseline. (Conclusion p.2, p.9)

questions.stem: What does the deck say a long change propagation represents?

options.text: A problem that it would be advantageous to shorten, e.g. by refactoring

questions.explanation: A long propagation is a problem; refactoring (move code into fewer classes, split roles) can shorten it. It is not ideal or required. (Drawlets p.31)

questions.stem: The move-the-code refactoring combines which two Fowler refactorings?

options.text: Extract Method and Pull Up Method

questions.explanation: Move-the-code extracts moveFigure (Extract Method) and pulls it up into ConstructionTool (Pull Up Method). The other pairs are not used here. (Drawlets p.33)

questions.stem: During actualization's test work, what is done for the new classes OwnerIdentity and SimpleListener?

options.text: Their tests are deferred to the next change
New unit tests are added for them

questions.explanation: Actualization adds new unit tests for the new classes, new functional tests for the new functionality, and updates impacted old tests. (Drawlets p.28)

questions.stem: Beyond having a certified baseline, what forces 'substantial extra work' before a release?

options.text: Because the baseline must be rewritten per user
Because the bug stack must be emptied first
Because delivering to real users means packaging, install/patching, and business coordination

questions.explanation: Release adds work because the certified baseline must be packaged and delivered to users (install or patch) as a technical-and-business decision. The baseline is not rewritten per user. (Conclusion p.9-10)

questions.stem: What does change propagation do when one class is changed?

options.text: Visits each newly-inconsistent neighbour and changes-or-confirms it until consistency is restored

questions.explanation: Change propagation visits each class made inconsistent by the change, changing or confirming it (which can spread further) until no inconsistencies remain. (Drawlets p.16-24)

questions.stem: Which best states why the baseline is both technical and social?

options.text: Technically it is a synchronisation point; socially, management tracks who misses the deadline and may require explanations
Socially only; it has no technical role
It is neither; it is purely a tool

questions.explanation: The baseline is a technical synchronisation point AND a social/accountability mechanism (management tracks misses and may ask for explanations). (Conclusion p.5)

questions.stem: Which statement about the merge tool is correct?

options.text: It incorporates small-release patches into the user's program

questions.explanation: The merge tool incorporates small-release patches into the user's program. It does not certify baselines, run acceptance tests, or locate concepts. (Conclusion p.10)

questions.stem: Why do both JHotDraw and Drawlets work well for teaching maintenance?

options.text: They are well-patterned OO drawing frameworks whose structure makes propagation paths legible
They have no tests, so changes are easy

questions.explanation: Both are well-patterned OO drawing frameworks (Composite figures, Strategy tools, Observer listeners); good patterning makes propagation paths legible. (Drawlets p.2; JHotDraw connection)

questions.stem: Reading the worked example, what is verification concretely embodied by?

options.text: The test suite (385 unit + 141 functional tests, run throughout and after)

questions.explanation: Verification, the band wrapping every phase, is concretely the test suite: 385 unit and 141 functional tests, run throughout and after the change. (Drawlets p.25-29)

questions.stem: What does the Initiation phase require, at minimum, for the owner change?

options.text: A change request that exists and is deemed worth doing

questions.explanation: Initiation requires a worth-doing request to exist; locating the class is concept location, the baseline is conclusion, and the test count is verification. (Drawlets p.6)

questions.stem: What is the business rationale that justifies initiating the owner change?

options.text: It makes SimpleApplet more versatile and adds support for cooperative work

questions.explanation: The rationale is that an owner makes SimpleApplet more versatile/useful and supports cooperative work - the case that justifies initiation. (Drawlets p.6)

questions.stem: Which concept is classified as 'irrelevant' generic English in the Drawlets table?
questions.explanation: modify (with implement, user, allowed, beginning, input, created) is irrelevant generic English. figure/canvas are significant; owner is external. (Drawlets p.8)

questions.stem: Among the figure types the deck lists for Drawlets, which one appears?
questions.explanation: Drawlets supports lines, freehand, rectangles, rounded rectangles, triangles, pentagons, polygons, ellipses, text boxes, and images. Charts/spreadsheets are not figure types. (Drawlets p.1)

questions.stem: What does 'baseline testing' primarily guarantee?

options.text: Progress, not regression (and as bug-free as possible)

questions.explanation: Baseline testing guarantees the baseline is as bug-free as possible and represents progress, not a regression. Build speed, stakeholder count, and release size are unrelated. (Conclusion p.3)

questions.stem: Summed up in a sentence, what does the Drawlets change request ask for?

options.text: Give each figure an owner so only the owner may modify it

questions.explanation: The request is to implement an owner per figure (the user who placed it) so only the owner may modify it, with ID/password at session start. (Drawlets p.6)

questions.stem: In the conclusion, which step certifies the integrated code as the team's new agreed version?
questions.explanation: Creating a new baseline (via thorough testing) certifies the integrated code as the new agreed reference. Commit only hands it to the team; release ships to users. (Conclusion p.2-3)

questions.stem: In the three-audience model, baseline certifies the code for which audience?
questions.explanation: Commit reaches the team, baseline certifies for the project, release ships to users. The merge tool only delivers patches. (Conclusion p.2, p.9)

questions.stem: Which step hands code to the team rather than the project or users?
questions.explanation: Commit hands code to the team. Baseline certifies for the project; release ships to users; acceptance is the stakeholder gate. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: What does the new SimpleListener class do in the owner change?

options.text: Wires the owner identity in as an Observer/listener

questions.explanation: SimpleListener is an Observer/listener that wires the owner identity into the design. It does not test, store figures, or deliver releases. (Drawlets p.16)

questions.stem: Which is the FIRST step of concept location on the owner request?
questions.explanation: Concept location first extracts concepts from the request, then classifies and searches. Marking the result, testing, and committing come later. (Drawlets p.7)

questions.stem: Concept classification drops each extracted concept into one of three buckets. Which three are they?
questions.explanation: Concepts are classified as irrelevant, external, or significant. The other triples are difficulty, colours, or conclusion steps. (Drawlets p.8)

questions.stem: Which conclusion step is a hard deadline to commit?
questions.explanation: The moment baseline testing starts is a hard deadline to commit; miss it and you wait for the next baseline. (Conclusion p.5)

questions.stem: Bugs in committed code typically surface during which activity?
questions.explanation: Faulty committed code reveals its bugs during baseline testing; earlier phases precede commit. (Conclusion p.6)

questions.stem: Roughly how large is the whole Drawlets framework?
questions.explanation: Drawlets is ~40 000 LOC with >100 classes and 35 interfaces. 91 is the change; 4800 is test-code LOC. (Drawlets p.2)

questions.stem: The two ways refactoring shortens propagation are moving code into fewer classes and what?

options.text: Splitting the roles of an overloaded method

questions.explanation: Refactoring shortens propagation by moving code into fewer classes and splitting overloaded-method roles. Deleting tests and skipping baselines are never proposed. (Drawlets p.31)

questions.stem: What does acceptance testing let stakeholders do at the end?
questions.explanation: Acceptance testing lets stakeholders approve software for release (and informs them of progress). Committing, baselining, and locating are other actors' jobs. (Conclusion p.8)
